data.title: Why Nike's Bruised Stock Might Be A Long-Term Winner
data.description:
At the time of writing this article, NIKE (NYSE:NKE) is trading for around $55 a share, and this price has been dropping for almost the past four years. While I recently wrote about broader market opportunities and why it is a compelling time to consider investing in the market as a whole, now may be a time to consider this iconic business at a beaten-down price.

While the company ballooned in stock price in the post-COVID-19 boom, the price has fallen so much that I thought it warranted a closer look to see if it has come too low and may give us a good entry point from a value investor's perspective to hold for the long term. At a P/E ratio of around 18, it seems that the market may be discounting Nike's brand power, which continues to be visible almost everywhere, from apparel, shoes, athletic equipment, and nearly anything that can be branded.

For context, Nike’s current forward P/E of around 25–26 is higher than that of Adidas, which is about 22, and significantly above Crocs, which trades at just under 10. On Holding, one of Nike’s fastest-growing competitors, has a forward P/E of roughly 40, which reflects aggressive forward growth expectations. While Nike isn’t the cheapest, the relative premium may be justified given the scale, profitability, and brand strength.

A bit of confidence can be gleaned from the fact that director Robert Swan recently purchased about $500,000 worth of shares. While this doesn't guarantee anything in terms of future capital appreciation, it can serve as a strong clue that there is no red flag that insiders are aware of internally, at least from the perspective of this particular director. Don't forget that insiders sell for many reasons, but they only buy for one reason: they think the price will go up.

More importantly, Nike's profitability remains strong, with a gross margin of around 44%, which, despite recent inventory challenges, is within the company's historical range. Also notable is that there is no deterioration in the return on invested capital (ROIC), which stands out at about 20% against a weighted average cost of capital (WACC) of about 10%. This healthy spread tells us that Nike continues to create excess value for shareholders against any cost of borrowing capital, and that management is deploying capital effectively. It's a reminder that this business model continues to work well for shareholders even during these tough times of strategic uncertainty.

I am typically not a dividend-focused investor, but it is also noteworthy that Nike's current dividend yield sits at about 2.8%. With a dividend payout ratio of roughly 50%, this seems to be a healthy dividend that is well covered by earnings with the potential to grow over the long term. While this isn't a massive number that shouldn't sway too many people, especially when looking at value investment ideas, it does give a pot it does give a potential it does give a potential investor some return while they are waiting for while they are waiting for future while they are waiting for future growth.

Revenue growth is another area that really doesn't show a crisis unfolding, with Nike in its global position where it stands. While there has been a slight decline over the past year, revenues have consistently grown for many years, with a three-year revenue growth rate of around 6.7%. This is not a spectacular number, but it continues to be a healthy growth rate for a company of this size. Another important factor is the substantial cash position with about $8.5 billion in the bank, giving management flexibility to navigate supply chain disruption and geopolitics and continue investing in product innovation. So far, all of this tells us that Nike should have the ability to be proactive rather than reactive like many of its competitors.

Tariffs, Inventory and Market Sentiment

The big elephant in the room for Nike right now, as it is for many other companies, is the aggressive shift in US tariff policy. With reciprocal tariffs that have hit countries like Vietnam and Indonesia, where much of Nike's product is made, there will be a near-term hit to profit margins. Suddenly facing 46% import tariffs from the region means that Nike will have to absorb these higher costs or try to pass those along to consumers. While Nike has pricing power, these are not ideal options as the economy slows and consumer sentiment is fragile.

Having said that, all of Nike's competitors are facing these same challenges. Virtually all major footwear and apparel makers are dependent on imported products, which reduces the relative impact of these problems in a competitive sense. While there is short-term pressure for Nike, a long-term path to diversifying manufacturing to lower-tariff regions is possible, or more favourable terms can be negotiated. Nike has the scale, supplier relationships, and financial capacity to navigate these issues in ways that smaller competitors cannot.

At the same time, Nike has aggressively been liquidating inventory, which is a move that is painful in the short term, but that will stabilize future profitability. This can be seen in recent earnings reports, which indicate that while inventory has come down year-over-year, it remains high by historical standards. Management acknowledged the inventory cleanup process may take several more quarters to be fully realized, which means that we are unlikely to see improvement in margins or a more significant revenue growth before 2026.

Brand Power and Strategy Shifts

Despite all the difficulties discussed so far, we must remember what makes and has made Nike valuable in the first place, and that is the brand. Nike is not just another clothing company, as it is arguably one of the strongest consumer brands in the world. It is an identity that has been built through decades of storytelling, athlete partnerships, and cultural relevance over the years, and this doesn't evaporate overnight. While there have always been strong competitors, including ones that have been gaining on Nike, such as On and Hoka, Nike still commands a piece of people's minds globally. With product lines deeply embedded in fashion, fitness and youth culture, it is in many ways the envy of its competition.

According to CEO Elliott Hill, Nike is currently undergoing a strategic reset under the "Win Now" plan. This will focus on streamlining core categories such as running, basketball, sportswear, football and training while at the same time reducing its dependence on classic product franchises such as Air Force 1 and Dunk. This is a pivot away from deeper discounting that will move excess inventory to factory outlets, which will help preserve the full-price image of the Nike brand across all channels. Further to this, Nike looks to be rekindling wholesale relationships after years of pushing direct-to-consumer (DTC) sales. This is quite notable, and while DTC will remain a higher margin model that won't disappear, wholesale partnerships will provide better consistency and visibility in a time of uneven demand. This is a critical shift that will play out at large retailers, and we can already see it in places such as Dick's Sporting Goods.

Value and the Long-Term View

Here is the part where we look at valuation, where things can become more difficult to assess. While Nike is certainly nowhere near its very large PE multiple of 40 just a few years ago, it still may not be a screaming bargain based on certain metrics that we look at. One concern is that forward earnings estimates have come down sharply over the last 12 months, and analysts have said they are expecting flat revenue growth and earnings between $2.10 and $2.30 for fiscal 2025. This means the forward PE ratio is more like 24 to 26, which is still lower than it has been in recent years, but it is still fairly elevated compared to previous down cycles in Nike's history.

Having said that, there is an important difference between paying for a broken business and investing at a fair price in a great one that is temporarily facing headwinds, even if they are serious ones. In my view, Nike appears to be in the second category. This business remains very profitable, with over $8 billion in cash. It also continues generating billions of dollars in free cash flow while raising the dividend annually for over a decade. If you believe that consumer spending will normalize and that Nike will eventually regain its place in China and North America, the current valuation may not be a bad place to begin accumulating a position for a long-term portfolio.

The Risks

While Nike presents a compelling long-term case for patient investors, there are numerous risks that could impact the company. First of all, there is continued market share erosion taking place to brands like On Holding and Hoka, which may continue if consumer preferences are not addressed. Second, if China-U.S. relations deteriorate further, new or extended tariffs would harm Nike’s margins and slow growth in this critical market. While Nike’s brand remains powerful, any major lag or misreading of the consumer and in product innovation or marketing could damage the brand if it is large enough. Finally, there is execution risk that is tied to the current turnaround strategy that must be acknowledged. If the "Win Now" initiative fails to reignite demand as promised, or a reduction in dependence on aging franchises is not taken seriously, earnings growth could disappoint further.

Conclusion: Focus On Long-Term Quality

Today, Nike sits at a crossroads where it is battered by the macroeconomics of the world, hurt by tariff policy, and still trying to figure out its operational imperfections. Despite recent declines in its price, it still appears to be a premium company with solid and durable competitive advantages that will carry it into the future. With a high return on capital, strong cash flow and a globally recognized iconic brand, investors have plenty of reasons to stay engaged and optimistic. This would not be a short-term look at the company, and investors also need to bet on the current strategic turnaround. In addition to normalization in global trade, Nike can reassert itself as a dominant global business, and the stock price will likely follow. There is no guarantee that this will be a smooth ride for shareholders, but for a patient investor, Nike offers a compelling entry point for a high-quality business.

data.title: Arcutis Opportunity To Capitalize On Leadership, Pipeline, And Commercial Assets
data.description:
Arcutis Biotherapeutics (NASDAQ:ARQT), a commercialized immuno-dermatology company with three approved products and a moderately sized development pipeline, is in a rather unique position that presents the company an opportunity to capitalize on.

  • CFO David Topper is transitioning.
  • IP and patent litigation on Zoryve has stayed.
  • Zoryve performing well above expectations.
  • Expect numerous clinical readouts and regulatory events through the year.

We believe that all the points listed above actually spell a positive impact on the company, although many may not view a CFO leaving as so. Nonetheless, we are optimistic about 2025 for Arcutis and assign a buy rating with a $19 price target for the company.

New CFO, Who This?

Last week, Arcutis announced that the Chief Financial Officer, David Topper, will be stepping down from his position after the company announces their 1Q 2025 financial results. This is just barely one year since David joined the company. Now David has a very impressive background in finance with a history of banking, corporate operations, capital raising, and more. However, we don't think that his strengths are a proper fit for a commercial stage biotech company that does not plan to raise additional capital at this time. After renegotiating the company's debt facility, we believe that projects that fit his expertise are thinning out at the company.

Arcutis has mentioned that the company expects:

  • Cash flow break-even in 2026
  • No engagement with equity markets to raise capital
  • No change in priorities for capital allocation
  • $226M in cash as of December 31, 2024

David's extensive experience in the capital markets seems to be a better fit for a pre-commercial biotech that is looking to raise capital, broaden banking and analyst relationships, leverage extensive relationships, and increase the institutional investor base. The company's statement says he is "retiring" from the position, but we will see if he will take up a better fitting role in the coming months.

While David will be stepping away, Latha Vairavan will be stepping in as CFO. With an extensive background in corporate finance and investor relations, we see Latha as a better "fit" for the current stage of the company as Arcutis moves towards cash flow break-even.

Litigation Low Down

Earlier this month, Arcutis and Padagis entered into an agreement to stay patent litigation on Zoryve (roflumilast). The two companies had a Markman Hearing scheduled for April 16th, but the stay nullified all trial dates currently set. As part of the agreement, Padagis will provide Arcutis any correspondence with the FDA from now on, which the companies can use to life the stay or leave as is.

From what we can find, all voluntarily agreed stays on abbreviated new drug application (ANDA) litigation cases resulted in settlements outside of the court. This spells positive news for Arcutis. This is because Padagis requested the stay to litigation.

While we will continue to monitor the ongoing litigation, which is required to update every 2 months, we expect Arcutis to come out on top when the dust settles.

Zoryve Zooms Past Expectations

In the most recent earnings release, Arcutis announced that Zoryve has exceeded analyst expectations with 4Q and FY24 revenues of $63M and $160M. This performance came out ahead of analyst projections by more than 10% where demand for Zoryve was strong across all indications. In the 4Q 2024 call, management expressed confidence in current consensus sales for the full year 2025 at approximately $250M. Expenses should remain fairly stable, with R&D expenses remaining flat and SG&A increasing slightly in correlation to the increased commercial efforts for Zoryve.

With the partnership with Kowa Pharmaceuticals in North America, there are over 200 sales reps from the Kowa team in the field helping deliver the therapy to patients in the primary care setting. With a relatively recent start to their efforts, aiding in the 55% quarter-over-quarter growth in 4Q24, we expect a meaningful impact from the sales force throughout 2025.

Upcoming Milestones

Zoryve Cream

  • Growing Medicaid coverage with two additional states added, looking to expand into additional states through 2025.
  • sNDA for Zoryve cream 0.05% submitted for treatment of atopic dermatitis, in12345 children 2–5 years old, action date anticipated end of year.

Zoryve Foam

  • Upcoming May 2025 PDUFA date from the FDA for sNDA for scalp and body psoriasis based on ARRECTOR trials.

ARQ-255

  • Data from Phase 1b study of ARQ-255 for alopecia areata expected in coming months.

ARQ-234

  • Plan to submit an IND application in 2H 2025.

Financials and Valuation

Improving the balance sheet, Arcutis paid down $100M of its $200M debt facility that it renegotiated with David, putting the company in great financial positioning. With $250M+ revenue expected in 2025 and growing to $1B+ in 2030 (based on Bloomberg consensus), we see a strong growth and an opportunity to buy Arcutis while it is undervalued. During management discussions during earnings, leadership has mentioned that the company is expected to reach cash flow break-even next year, being able to fully fund its clinical development pipeline and continue to progress its sales force without the need of additional outside capital.

With Arcutis's current clinical and commercial stage status, we utilize a WACC of 12% and terminal growth rate of 3% and an 70% POS for regulatory outcomes. With these assumptions along with cash and revenue projections, we can arrive at a 2025 price target of $19 per share. We are impressed with Arcutis's strong gross margins and revenue growth and hope to see these trends continue in the years to come.

Risk

While unlikely due to the strong position of Arcutis and Padagis initiating the litigation stay, ongoing legal battles over the IP remains a risk. With the ability of Padagis to lift the stay at any time, we will continue to watch the procession for meaningful updates that may negatively (or positively) impact the stock.

Cash constraints may also prove an issue if sales of Zoryve don't continue the current trajectory and falter. With approximately 1 year of cash should nothing change from current operations, Arcutis may run into the need to finance before reaching cash flow break-even next year. We view this as a small risk due to the ongoing sales performance of Zoryve. The more likely risk to sales is a lack of improvement in gross-to-net, which has seen a movement towards steady-state to the low 50%.

We see little growing competitive risk to Zoryve, as the therapy is the most prescribed non-steroidal topical treatment in three different inflammatory skin conditions. With the continued growth, growing sales force, and physician uptake, we don't see Zoryve losing any market share it takes.

Conclusion

We assign a buy rating to Arcutis with a 2025 price target of $19 per share. We are excited to see the revenue growth from Zoryve which, we think, will continue to beat estimates now that we have the full force of the Kowa sales team behind it. With a new CFO who, we think, is a better fit for this stage and size of company to lead financial operations and a strong balance sheet that is set to reach break-even in the next year, we are very optimistic. We look forward to continued pipeline updates through the year and hearing the outcomes from FDA interactions in the coming months.

data.title: DFP: Solid Fund But Wrong Time For Entry
data.description:
Overview

Flaherty & Crumrine Dynamic Preferred and Income Fund (NYSE:DFP) operates as a closed end fund that aims to generate attractive total returns from its diverse portfolio of preferred investments. Preferred investments tend to be of higher quality and usually provide consistent income. During this period of market uncertainty, a fund like DFP could be an enticing pick to offset volatility. DFP is an interesting investment because a significant portion of its distributions are classified as qualified dividends. The fund has a public inception dating back to 2013, meaning we have over a decade worth of performance history to reference.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that DFP's price has increased by 7.4%, despite the recent decline caused by President Trump's tariff proposals. When including all distributions paid out to shareholders, the total return is nearly 15.2% over the same period. DFP could be a great choice for income investors since the distributions are paid out on a monthly basis. Additionally, the fund has been successful at generating a sufficient amount of earnings to support the payouts.

Since the portfolio contains preferred securities, DFP's price has demonstrated sensitivity to the current interest rate environment. This has caused DFP to now trade at a sizeable discount to NAV valuation, which could indicate that this is a good time for entry. However, I remain cautious about the effects of a prolonged period of elevated rates. It doesn't seem like a meaningful amount of interest rate cuts are going to happen anytime soon, so DFP's price may remain suppressed. So let's start by taking a look at the fund's underlying strategy and investment breakdown.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fact sheet, DFP has net assets of $458M that is spread across approximately 250 holdings. We can see that Banks make up the largest portion of DFP's portfolio at 56.4%. This is followed by an allocation to Insurance and Utility investments, accounting for 18.8% and 11.6% of assets respectively. However, the combination of Bank and Insurance investments makes up over 75% of the fund's assets, so we can assume that these will have the greatest impact on DFP's daily price changes and overall total return potential.

The fund expands upon its diversity by also providing some global exposure to markets outside of the US. Therefore, DFP could be a way to slowly start diversifying your income sources outside of US securities. The breakdown is as follows:

  • United States: 67.9%
  • United Kingdom: 8.3%
  • Canada: 8.2%
  • France: 6%
  • Spain: 4.8%
  • Bermuda: 1.4%
  • Mexico: 1.3%
  • Ireland: 0.9%
  • Switzerland: 0.5%
  • Australia: 0.5%
  • Netherlands: 0.2%
  • Germany: 0.1%

The fund utilizes leverage as part of its strategy in an effort to amplify returns when an attractive opportunity presents itself. DFP currently has leverage in the amount of 38.7% of assets, which I find to be pretty aggressive during this period of elevated interest rates. Since higher interest rates directly translate to a higher cost of debt maintenance, DFP needs to ensure that this leverage is yielding positive results. Investors should consider the risk that leverage can also amplify losses if allocated towards a poor investment. Ideally, I would like to see leverage come down to at least below 30% while interest rates are high.

When observing the top issuers within DFP, we can see many notable names of some large-cap companies. For instance, Citigroup (C) is the largest individual issuer. This is followed by varying weights to other notable names like Morgan Stanley (MS), MetLife (MET), and Goldman Sachs (GS) just to name a few.

Valuation

Since DFP operates as a closed end fund, the price can trade at a difference from the underlying value of its assets. As a result, the price currently trades at a discount to NAV of about 9.4%. For reference, DFP traded at an average discount to NAV of nearly 7.8% over the last three-year period, indicating that the current discount is attractive. Looking over the last ten years below, we can see that DFP once traded in the premium to NAV territory between 2020 and 2021. Coincidentally, this was the period when interest rates were cut to near zero levels in response to the pandemic.

Despite interest rates weighing down the price to NAV valuation, the fund has demonstrated solid fundamentals. For instance, net investment income was $1.26 per share while net realized gains amounted to $2.71 per share for 2024. This resulted in a net increase of $3.97 per share, which left plenty of excess after the distributions were paid out. As a result, DFP has demonstrated sizeable NAV growth since its low of last year, despite these unfavorable conditions. For instance, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2020: $26.51
  • 2021: $26.83
  • 2022: $20.86
  • 2023: $19.70
  • 2024: $22.36

Once interest rates start to come down, I believe that DFP will be able to capture a greater amount of NAV growth over time. For now, I do think that resilience should be commended. Looking back at the performance since inception, we can see that DFP's price only took a downturn once interest rates were increased throughout 2022.

Risk Profile

Investors should be aware that there is a portion of DFP's securities are rated below investment grade. For reference, ratings below a BBB are considered to be below investment grade, while anything between a AAA to BBB is classified as investment grade. The breakdown according to the fund's overview on credit quality is as follows:

  • AAA: 0%
  • AA: 0%
  • A: 0.4%
  • BBB: 47.8%
  • BB: 34.9%
  • B: 1.9%
  • Below B: 0.3%
  • Not Rated: 14.7%

A higher interest rate environment means that the cost of debt is elevated. DFP holds a sizeable amount of leverage, which could negatively impact the fund's earnings. Additionally, there's a chance that the lower rated securities within DFP's portfolio could go through a default.

Looking at the graph below, we can see the clear inverse relationship that DFP's price has shared with the federal funds rate. When rates were cut to near zero levels throughout 2020 and 2021, DFP's price rapidly increased. Conversely, DFP's price retracted to the downside when the Fed aggressively hiked interest rates to decade highs throughout 2022 and 2023.

Therefore, future interest rates can serve as a catalyst for price growth in the future. The only variable is that we don't necessarily know how aggressive rate cuts may be or when they'll actually happen. It seems like the Fed is waiting to see the effects of the tariffs that President Trump has put into place before making a decision. However, inflationary data remains higher than anticipated, which could delay the pace at which rates are cut. Depending on which direction inflation goes, there's even a chance that interest rates are increased again, which would drive DFP's price lower.

Dividend

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.1174 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 7%. If we multiply this monthly payout by twelve, we can have an estimated annual payout of $1.4088 per share. Referring back to the same annual report, DFP has had no issues generating earnings in excess of this distribution amount. We can see that the monthly payouts were slightly reduced throughout 2023. However, the current payout is so conservative that I do not believe management has a reason to implement additional cuts, assuming that interest rates do not get cut. Depending on which direction interest rates go, there is a possibility that the payouts can get reduced if the price falls.

One of the most notable aspects of DFP is that 98% of the distributions were classified as qualified dividend income. So despite all of the macroeconomic challenges the fund has to navigate, this is one of the most attractive aspects of the fund because it enables retired investors to collect a high income with little tax consequences.

Qualified dividends have more favorable tax treatment compared to the ordinary dividends you may receive from other high-yield funds. This makes it possible for DFP to be utilized outside of traditional tax advantaged accounts. However, I would consult with a tax professional to determine what the best use case for your specific situation.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I rate DFP as a hold for now due to the uncertainty around the interest rate environment. We have yet to see the effects of the tariffs and inflation remains higher than anticipated, so I believe there's a chance that the Fed will raise interest rates. This would ultimately drive DFP's price lower, which can present a more attractive opportunity for entry. Additionally, the fund utilizes a large amount of leverage, which could slow the fund's growth in a higher interest rate environment. Despite these challenges, DFP has demonstrated NAV resilience and strong earnings in excess of its distributions. I like the fund's diversity, global exposure, and qualified dividends that it provides. I just don't think that entry here is the most ideal.
